glossary.def: An optimizer modification that accumulates an exponentially-decaying running average of past gradients (a velocity) and steps along it. Consistent directions reinforce while oscillating ones cancel; for a steady gradient the effective step grows by 1/(1−μ), a 10× amplification at the usual μ = 0.9.

An adaptive optimizer that keeps a first moment (gradient mean) and a second moment (mean squared gradient), bias-corrects both, and divides the step by the root of the second moment. The result gives each parameter a roughly scale-invariant step, which is why it is the default for transformers and most modern deep nets.

Adam with decoupled weight decay: the decay term λθ is applied directly to the weights, outside the adaptive rescaling, rather than as an L2 penalty folded into the loss. Decoupling restores true weight decay and consistently generalizes better, making AdamW the standard transformer optimizer.

Linearly ramping the learning rate from zero to its peak over the first 1–5% of training steps. It lets an optimizer's adaptive statistics and a network's fragile early layers stabilize before full-size updates, preventing the early divergence that large initial steps can cause.

Decaying the learning rate from its peak to a floor along a half-cosine curve after warmup — gentle at first, steepest in the middle, flattening at the end. Its slow start buys exploration and its slow finish lets the model settle; it reliably beats step decay on large models.

A regularizer that zeroes each activation independently with probability p during training and rescales survivors by 1/(1−p) so the expected activation is unchanged. It prevents co-adaptation of neurons and approximates training an ensemble of subnetworks; it is disabled at inference.

Running most computation in 16-bit (fp16 or bf16) for speed and memory while keeping an fp32 master copy of the weights and fp32 reductions for numerical safety. fp16 needs loss scaling to avoid gradient underflow; bf16 keeps fp32's exponent range and usually needs none.

Multiplying the loss by a large factor S before backprop so every gradient is scaled by S, lifting tiny values out of fp16's underflow region, then dividing the gradients by S before the weight update. Dynamic loss scaling tunes S automatically, halving it whenever an inf/NaN appears.

refs.cite: Kingma, D. P. & Ba, J. (2014). Adam: A Method for Stochastic Optimization. ICLR 2015.
Loshchilov, I. & Hutter, F. (2017). Decoupled Weight Decay Regularization (AdamW). ICLR 2019.
Micikevicius, P. et al. (2017). Mixed Precision Training. ICLR 2018.
Loshchilov, I. & Hutter, F. (2016). SGDR: Stochastic Gradient Descent with Warm Restarts. ICLR 2017.
Srivastava, N. et al. (2014). Dropout: A Simple Way to Prevent Neural Networks from Overfitting. JMLR 15.
Keskar, N. S. et al. (2016). On Large-Batch Training for Deep Learning: Generalization Gap and Sharp Minima. ICLR 2017.
Goodfellow, I., Bengio, Y. & Courville, A. (2016). Deep Learning. MIT Press.
